Name: curvature_aware_mixed_precision
Title: Curvature-Aware Mixed Precision Quantization for Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating curvature-aware optimization and spectral quantization theory into mixed precision quantization can significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of neural networks. By leveraging the geometric properties of the loss landscape, we can adaptively allocate precision to the most sensitive parts of the network, reducing the impact of quantization noise and improving overall performance.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored training-free mixed precision quantization, adaptive mixed-precision post-training quantization, and fine-grained adaptive mixed-precision training frameworks. However, these approaches do not explicitly consider the curvature of the loss landscape, which is critical in understanding the sensitivity of the network to quantization noise. Our approach distinguishes itself by incorporating curvature-aware optimization and spectral quantization theory to provide a more principled and efficient method for mixed precision quantization.
Abstract: Mixed precision quantization has emerged as a promising technique for reducing the computational cost and memory requirements of neural networks. However, existing approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods or uniform precision allocation, which can lead to suboptimal performance. In this paper, we propose a curvature-aware mixed precision quantization framework that adaptively allocates precision to the most sensitive parts of the network. By leveraging the geometric properties of the loss landscape, our approach reduces the impact of quantization noise and improves overall performance. We evaluate our framework on a range of benchmark datasets and demonstrate significant improvements in accuracy and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on a range of benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10, using popular neural network architectures such as ResNet and MobileNet. We will evaluate the performance of our curvature-aware mixed precision quantization framework against state-of-the-art baselines, including EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP. We will use metrics such as top-1 accuracy, FLOPs, and memory usage to assess the efficiency and effectiveness of our approach. We will also conduct ablation studies to investigate the impact of different components of our framework, such as the curvature-aware optimization and spectral quantization theory.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is the computational cost of computing the curvature of the loss landscape, which can be expensive for large neural networks. To mitigate this, we will explore efficient approximations and parallelization techniques to reduce the computational overhead. Another limitation is the potential for overfitting to the curvature of the loss landscape, which can lead to poor generalization performance. To address this, we will use regularization techniques and early stopping to prevent overfitting.
